ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002572); no eggplant-specific biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers biryani as a rice dish category; no eggplant-specific biryani code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for eggplant biryani or biryani as a food item in the clinical terminology.

main_ingredients: whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon)
ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of baigan biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice layered with cooked eggplant, typically served with raita or pickle on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), adapted with USDA FoodData Central reference values for basmati rice and eggplant components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN (2017) provides nutrient data for vegetable biryani as a composite dish. Eggplant biryani is a recognised variant; values are adapted from the vegetable biryani entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked basmati rice (FDC ID 169704) and cooked eggplant (FDC ID 169228) to confirm macro plausibility. Per-100 g values reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation with ghee, yogurt, and aromatic spices.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice biryani
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for eggplant biryani. GI of 57 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice biryani (GI ~57, medium category), which is the closest analogous dish. Eggplant has a very low GI (~15) and its inclusion does not materially raise the dish GI above the rice-dominant baseline. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 82.25) / 100 ≈ 47.0, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 23.5 × 3.5 = 82.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spices, salt, and ghee in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g restaurant serving of baigan biryani contributes approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing over 47% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and high glycemic load per full serving due to basmati rice content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI of 57 and high glycemic load (~47 per 350 g serving) mean that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 150–200 g, pair with a high-fibre side such as raita or salad, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from ghee and yogurt; eggplant provides dietary fibre and antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baigan biryani contains approximately 1.8 g saturated fat per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose preparations using minimal ghee or substitute with a plant-based oil, and benefit from the soluble fibre and nasunin antioxidant content of eggplant.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baigan (Eggplant) Biryani is a fragrant Indian vegetarian rice dish in which tender pieces of eggplant (brinjal or aubergine, known as baigan or baingan in Hindi) are slow-cooked with whole and ground aromatic spices — including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala — and layered with long-grain basmati rice that has been partially cooked in spiced water. The dish is finished using the dum method, in which the pot is sealed and cooked over low heat so that the steam from the eggplant, yogurt marinade, and rice mingles to create a unified, deeply flavoured dish. The eggplant becomes soft and absorbs the spice mixture, while the basmati rice remains fluffy and separate. Ghee or oil is used to sauté the aromatics and enrich the dish. Fresh mint and coriander leaves, fried onions, and saffron-infused milk or food colouring are typically added as finishing layers. The result is a dish with a complex, earthy, mildly spiced flavour profile and a soft, moist texture. Nutritionally, baigan biryani provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with 23.5 g carbohydrate, 3.8 g protein, and 4.2 g fat. The eggplant contributes dietary fibre (1.8 g per 100 g), potassium, and the antioxidant nasunin. The yogurt adds calcium and a small amount of protein. The dish has a medium glycemic index of approximately 57 due to the basmati rice base, and a high glycemic load per full restaurant serving, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations due to added salt and spices. Baigan biryani is suitable for vegetarians and is commonly halal. It is served as a main course in Indian restaurants across Singapore, South Asia, and the South Asian diaspora globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in eggplant biryani?
Is brinjal biryani good for diabetics?
What is the GI of vegetable biryani?
How much sodium is in biryani?
What spices are used in baingan biryani?
Can I eat biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between baigan biryani and regular biryani?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for eggplant biryani exists; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked basmati rice and cooked eggplant. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice biryani. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient values are adapted rather than directly measured for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) vegetable biryani data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component entries; no exact eggplant biryani entry was found in a primary source. 2. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice biryani entry; eggplant's own very low GI (~15) does not materially alter the dish GI. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data for the constituent ingredients under typical preparation conditions. 6. Sodium value of 310 mg per 100 g reflects a typical restaurant-style preparation with added salt; home preparations may be significantly lower. 7. The food is served at Briyani Hut – Boat Quay on GrabFood Singapore at SGD 10.90. 8. FoodOn match type is closest_parent (general biryani class); no eggplant-specific biryani subclass exists in FoodOn. 9. FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent; no eggplant-specific biryani code exists. 10. SNOMED CT match type is none; no suitable clinical terminology code found.
